title: Metastelma suberosum
intro: Metastelma suberosum, also known as the "stiff-leaved metastelma", is a perennial climber in the Apocynaceae family of plants, native to tropical Africa. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in treating various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of M. suberosum include alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, M. suberosum is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used to make ointments and salves, particularly for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects.
taxonomy: Metastelma suberosum is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, with Metastelma suberosum belonging to the subfamily Asclepiadoideae. The genus Metastelma is a group of flowering plants native to tropical and subtropical regions, with Metastelma suberosum being one of the species found in these regions. Metastelma suberosum is an evergreen shrub or climber, and its classification reflects its unique characteristics and adaptations to its environment. Overall, the classification of Metastelma suberosum provides valuable insights into its evolutionary history and relationships with other plants.
uses: Metastelma suberosum is reported to have various medicinal uses, including its application in the treatment of fever and diarrhea. It is also believed to possess antimicrobial properties, which can aid in the healing of wounds and the prevention of infections.
